english: fusing Christian knowledge by means of books and tracts.

Tar. 91, 7. 10. Transl. p. 317: 'acc. to Was. a rapacious mountain tribe, north-east of Tibet; in the Tibetan-Sanskrit dictionary mentioned as 'Turushka"'. They are doubtless the same robbers, that are called 'Kolo' by Huc (II. p. 187), who were known to our Lama from Tashilhunpo as mgo-lóg (mgo log), or lcaṅ-mo-mgo-lóg (lcang mo mgo log), they having received this name ('queer-heads') in consequence of having their hair closely cropped. Possibly gar-lóg (gar log) is the older and more correct form; cf. dar-rgyas-gliṅ (dar rgyas gling).
the muscles of the thumb (?) Med.

1. importance, gál-du ¸dzín-pa (gal du 'dzin pa) to consider of importance, to esteem highly Mil.; gál-ċan (gal can) Cs., more frq. gal-ċé-ba (gal che ba) important, de mi śin-tu gál-ċé-bar yód-do (de mi shin tu gal che bar yod do) Glr., bsláb-bya gal-ċé-ba (bslab bya gal che ba) Glr. important precepts; gal-ċuṅ (gal chung) unimportant, insignificant; undervalued, slighted Mil.; gál-po (gal po) prob. = gal (gal), Schr.; gal-po-ċé-yi bzá-dpon (gal po che yi bza' dpon) the important, indispensable master of the house Mil. - 2. constraint, compulsion, *ṅa-la ghal ḫuṅ* (*nga la ghal hung*) C. I have been compelled. - 3. trap, snare C., W., also Mil.; *gal-ltém* (*gal ltem*) W. id.; gal ¸dzúg-pa (gal 'dzug pa) to set a trap or snare.
Med. ?
W. crow-bar, handspike.

I. sbst., gál-te mċán-kuṅ bcùṅ (gal te mchan kung bcung) Pth.? -- II. conj. if, in case, serves to introduce a conditional sentence, ending with na (na) (which is the essential word, whereas gal-te (gal te) may be left out as well): gál-te .. ¸óṅ-na (gal te .. 'ong na) if .. comes (ἐὰν ... ἔλθῃ); also followed by yaṅ (yang) (kyaṅ (kyang)), although black snow fell Dzl. (nas (nas) instead of na (na), frq. to be met with, is either merely a slip of the pen, or an impropriety of speech). -- gál-te-na (gal te na) as one word, and with the signification of perhaps, or the Greek ἄν (not 'if', Cs.) I found only in a few passages of the Kye-lang manuscript of Dzl., where the edition of Sch. has gál-te (gal te), which makes no sense. gal-srid (gal srid) W. = gal-te (gal te). In Lewin's Manual it often occurs in the sense of but, however.
n. of a disease Med.
to force, to press something on a person (cf. gal (gal) 2), mi-la btson gal (mi la btson gal) in-door confinement is forced on men Mil.
W. refuse, rubbish.
v. gás-pa (gas pa).
1. num. for 33. -- 2. affix instead of kyi (kyi), after g (g) and ṅ (ng); for the signification v. kyi (kyi).
the vowel-sign ི, i.

wylie: gi gu shel, gi gu sha
english: Sch.; 'having a white speck in the eye, wall-eyed (of horses)'.

Glr., gi-bám (gi bam) Lt., also giu (giu), or giu-wáṅ (giu wang), Cs.: 'n. of a concretion in the entrails of some animals, used for medicine'. But Glr. 35, 9 an elephant has it on its neck, and acc. to oral assertions it is to be found also in the human head; a man for instance, is said to have gi-wáṅ (gi wang) in his brains, if in his sleep he is heard to utter long-drawn humming sounds.
a strong-bodied, durable horse Sch.
Wts. a fabulous animal.
Pth. prob. a little drum, or the beating of it, as an accompaniment to dancing.
affix, v. kyin (kyin).
Ld. the Indian rupee, = 5 jau (jau).
instead of kyis (kyis) after g (g) and ṅ (ng), v. kyis (kyis).

1. num. for 63. -- 2. sign of diminutives, e.g. khyí-gu (khyi gu) puppy, little dog. -- 3. extension, extent, room, space rṅás-sa gu-dóg (rngas sa gu dog), lúṅ-pa gu-dóg (lung pa gu dog), lám gu-dóg (lam gu dog) a nar-
